name: Hidden Beach
description: A lovers' beach, tucked below the surface of the island, provides a safe haven for romance.

name: Cafebrería El Péndulo
description: Part bookstore, part café, and part forest.

name: La Casa Azul
description: Frida Kahlo's childhood home, now a museum of her life and works.

name: Biblioteca Vasconcelos
description: A jaw-dropping "megalibrary" that turns book-browsing into a geometric adventure.

name: Mercado de Sonora

name: El Nido de Quetzalcóatl

name: Soumaya Museum

name: Cenote Xkeken

name: Santuario Nacional del Angel de la Santa Muerte

name: Pyramid of Kukulcan at Chich'en Itza

name: The Mummies at Museo de El Carmen

name: Las Pozas
description: Extraordinary sculpture garden created by an eccentric English poet.

name: Minichelista Restaurant
description: A quirky and charming restaurant founded by artists.

name: The Sweets Section at Mercado de la Merced
description: Mexico City's largest market contains a Willy Wonka-esque wonderland.

name: Palacio Postal

description: These biodiverse university gardens form a green oasis amid the urban chaos of Mexico City.

name: Fuente de Tláloc
description: Diego Rivera's massive tiled fountain and failed infrastructure project.

name: La Rifa Chocolatería

name: Café La Habana

name: Mercado San Juan
description: CDMX's home for the world's culinary delights.

name: Taquería Los Cocuyos

name: Museo Dolores Olmedo

name: Museo Subacuático de Arte

name: Nautilus House

name: Chichen Itza Chirp

name: Palenque

name: Hierve El Agua

name: National Museum of Anthropology Murals
description: The unique collection often goes unnoticed by visitors focused only on the archaeological objects.

name: Disk of Death

name: Mariposario Chapultepec (Chapultepec Butterfly Garden)

name: Espacio Escultórico (Sculptural Space)

name: Museo Anahuacalli

name: Palace of the Inquisition

name: Centro de Cultura Digital

name: Chicxulub Crater

name: Monolith of Tlaloc

name: Museo de las Momias de Guanajuato

name: Temple of Ehecatl-Quetzalcoatl

name: Cenote Angelita

name: Xoloitzcuintles of the Dolores Olmedo Museum

name: Xoloitzcuintles of the Dolores Olmedo Museum
description: A small pack of an ancient, hairless breed of dog once believed to guide souls through the underworld lives on its grounds.

name: Aztec Serpent Head Cornerstone
description: On a cornerstone of the City Museum is the head of a monstrous serpent stolen from an Aztec pyramid 400 years ago.

name: Pyramid of Cuicuilco
description: This ancient structure was built by a mysterious civilization that was largely destroyed by a volcanic eruption.

name: Conch Shell House

name: Yaxchilan

name: El Árbol del Tule (The Tule Tree)

name: Plaza Santos Degollado

name: Franz Mayer Museum Courtyard

name: Tula Giants

name: Biblioteca Palafoxiana
description: The first public library in the Americas has more than 45,000 books dating back to the 15th century.

name: La Lobera

name: Undersea Brine Lake

name: Cineteca Nacional de Mexico

name: Cablebús Line 1

name: Macromural de Pachuca

name: La Pascualita

name: Moctezuma's Treasure

name: José Luis Cuevas Museum
description: Its most enigmatic sculpture is a giantess with a ghostly face on her knee.

name: Cascada El Chiflón

name: Centro Ceremonial Otomí
description: This ceremonial Mexican square looks as though it belongs in a science fiction movie.

name: Leonora Carrington Museum
description: This dreamlike museum is filled with the works of an influential surrealist artist whose name you may have never heard.

name: Guillermo Tovar de Teresa House Museum

name: Alley of the Kiss

name: Hotel Camino Real Polanco
description: This hotel is an amazing example of the Escuela Tapatía de Arquitectura movement.

name: Concrete Animal Cemetery

name: The Ex-Votos of Churubusco

name: 'El Perfil del Tiempo'

name: Rafael Coronel Museum
description: Mexico's largest collection of masks is found in the ruins of an abandoned convent.

name: Rufino Tamayo Museum of Pre-Hispanic Art
description: An incredible collection of Mesoamerican art assembled by one of Mexico's great artists.

name: 'Xipe Totec'

name: Baja Film Studios

name: Iguana Bridge
description: This bridge over the River Cuale leads to a neighborhood that was once an escape for Hollywood's elite.

name: National Center for the Arts (CENART)

name: La Vasconia
description: Founded by a Basque immigrant, Mexico City's oldest bakery has a history dating back to 1870.

name: Calixtlahuaca

name: Grutas de Cacahuamilpa

name: The Chapel of Jimmy Ray
description: An artist's suburban abode looks like an architectural fever dream.

name: Hacienda del Cochero
description: Peaceful gardens hide an underground torture museum.

name: Monte Albán

name: Parque Masayoshi Ohira (Masayoshi Ohira Park)

name: Temple of Santa Muerte
description: This church devoted to Mexico's "dead saint" is a festive celebration of a formerly hidden religious figure.

name: National Museum of Interventions
description: A bullet-scarred monastery chronicles Mexico's turbulent history of foreign invasions.

name: San Gervasio
description: The ruins of an important pilgrimage destination for worshippers of a Maya fertility goddess.

name: Flowers & Gardens Spring Festival
description: Mexico City decorates its facades with extravagant floral arrangements every year.

name: El Árbol Milenario
description: A fairytale-like grove with a crystal-clear spring bubbling under the shade of a massive ancient tree.

name: Murals of Bonampak
description: Three small rooms in the jungle house the most elaborate and intact murals of the Mayan world.

name: Pomuch Cemetery
description: Once a year in Pomuch, the dead are taken out for a cleaning.

name: Mercado Melchor Múzquiz Murals
description: Outside a market, beautiful murals pay tribute to fruit sellers and farmers, as well as a few legendary artists.

name: Mural of the Great Goddess
description: A striking depiction of Teotihuacan's mysterious "Spider Woman."

name: Isla Mujeres Sea-View Cemetery
description: An island graveyard with spectacular views of the Caribbean.

name: CCEMx Site Museum
description: The remains of an Aztec school and various intriguing artifacts lie below a modern cultural center.

name: Amparo Museum
description: This beautiful and unmissable little museum is full of fascinating artifacts from Mesoamerica's great civilizations.

name: La Escondida
description: A fairy garden in an old hotel hidden in La Marquesa forest.

name: Museo del Barro (Museum of Clay)
description: A collection of incredible clay crafts, complete with earthen skeletons and mermaids.

name: The Pyramid of El Cerrito
description: The ruins of an ancient pyramid built by the mysterious Chupícuaro civilization.

name: Murals of Cacaxtla
description: These strikingly colorful paintings were created by a mysterious Mesoamerican civilization.

name: Column of Death
description: A mysterious pillar in an ancient burial chamber is said to predict how long you have to live.

name: 'El Baile de los 41' ('Dance of the 41') Memorial Plaque
description: A tribute to the 20th-century ball that cast a spotlight on Mexico’s LGBTQ community.

name: Zócalo Metro Models
description: These subterranean models show Mexico City's central square during three different eras.

name: Cabeza de Juárez
description: This giant expressionist head crowns a museum devoted to a beloved Mexican leader.

name: House of the Eagles
description: The ruins of a former sanctuary for elite Aztec warriors.

name: Museo de la Radio (Radio Museum)
description: A Mexico City subway station boasts a hidden museum, complete with a working radio booth.

name: El Charco del Ingenio (The Mill Puddle)
description: This botanical garden boasts sculptures, industrial ruins, and an endorsement from the Dalai Lama.

name: Chinkultic
description: The Acropolis of this little-visited Mayan archaeological site offers great views of Chiapas’s Lake District.

name: Nieves Exóticas of Jardín Principal
description: Meat and seafood are part of the frozen dessert flavors available at these legendary street stands.

name: Metro Auditorio
description: This Mexico City subway station will make you feel like you've been transported to the London Underground.

name: The Pyramid of Santa Cecilia Acatitlán
description: This partially reconstructed Aztec pyramid gives a glimpse of what its larger counterparts would have looked like.

name: Festival of the Exploding Hammers
description: There is nothing like a religious festival celebrated with blow-up sledgehammers.

name: La Pasita
description: This cluttered cantina is famed for its cheese-spiked, raisin-flavored liqueur.

name: Franz Mayer Museum Silver Collection
description: A shining treasure trove of fine Mexican silver dating back to the 15th century.

name: Panteón de Belen (Santa Paula Cemetery)
description: This historic cemetery built for victims of the epidemics that swept Guadalajara is shrouded in macabre tales.

name: El Bunker de JP
description: In this beloved beach town, a secret seafood haven lies tucked behind a car wash.

name: The Animals of Parque España
description: Some charming, but sadly, unloved animal statues call this park home.

name: Cal y Maíz

name: Ex-Monastery of Santiago Apóstol

name: Cerro de las Campanas (Hill of Bells)

name: Museo Nacional de Cartografía

name: Iglesia de San Juan
description: The masses at this church incorporate enough moonshine and animal sacrifice that they are no longer recognizably Catholic.

name: 'Un Sueño Posible' ('A Possible Dream')

name: Fonoteca Nacional
description: Mexico's National Sound Library houses music, recordings, and voices from the nation's rich past.

name: Hotel Quinta Real
description: Hotel designed around a 16th century Mexican bullring.

name: Audiorama Parque España
description: This tiny garden was built specifically to provide a place of peace and serenity away from the busy city streets.

description: A giant Jesus statue watches over the city of Saltillo from the top of a mountain.

name: Beatriz de la Fuente Teotihuacán Murals Museum
description: These millennia-old murals once covered the walls of residential complexes across the ancient city.

name: Bodaega
description: This bakery forgoes industrial wheat in favor of locally harvested grains.

name: Biblioteca Pública de la Universidad Michoacana
description: This former 17th-century church now houses a university's public library along with several mural masterworks.

name: Museo de Ropa Étnica de México (Mexican Ethnic Clothing Museum)
description: This small museum explores Mexican history and culture through clothing.

name: Monument to the Independence of Chiapas
description: Also known as "The Book," this monument is dedicated to Chiapas's history of independence.

name: Durango Walk of Fame
description: A series of sculptures depict the actors who starred in the many cowboy movies shot in this Mexican city.

name: Laboratorio de Arte Alameda
description: A 16th-century convent became a contemporary art museum.

name: Benjamín Carrión Statue
description: An ode to a chess-loving diplomat.

name: Mexico Me Encanta! Miniature Museum
description: This detailed miniature model covers all aspects of life in a small Mexican village.

name: Centro de las Artes de San Agustín (Arts Center of San Agustín)
description: The former textile factory is now an oasis for the arts.

name: The Former Chapel Hospital of Los Juaninos
description: This colonial-era hospital chapel is now a foyer and side entrance to a popular department store.

name: Cenote Ziiz Ha
description: A cenote filled with Colonial era artifacts whose waters cannot be accessed.

name: Casa Reforma
description: Inside one of Guadalajara's oldest houses, you can find puppets and foam models of the city's missing buildings.

name: Tacos del Carmen
description: Don’t be surprised if you run into some of Oaxaca’s top chefs in line at this family run taco stop.

name: Galerías Plaza de las Estrellas
description: Likely Mexico City's most eclectic shopping mall, with its own version of the Walk of Fame.

name: Tlachtemalácatl (Mesoamerican Ballgame Stone Ring)
description: This ancient gaming tool was unearthed during the construction of this former town's central square.

name: Roberina Sidewalk Adverts
description: These mosaic designs promise a cure for headaches, toothaches, and colds.

name: Don Quixote Iconographic Museum
description: The world's largest collection of Don Quixote-inspired art.

name: Torre Insignia Carillon
description: The world's tallest carillon is located at the top of this skyscraper in Mexico City.

name: Rodolfo Morales Municipal Palace Mural
description: A little-known hall almost completely covered in art by the beloved local painter.

name: 'Vendedoras de Gorditas' ('Gorditas Saleswomen')
description: A tribute to a local delicacy and the women who prepare and sell it.

name: Iglesia de Santa Bárbara (Church of Saint Bárbara)
description: This metal church may or may not have been designed by Alexandre-Gustave Eiffel.

name: Little Boy Zero
description: Statue of Edgar Hernandez, the first victim of swine flu.

name: La Muelita (The Little Molar)
description: A small building that loosely resembles a tooth is appropriately used as a dentist office.

name: Parish of Our Lady of Guadalupe
description: This colorful, geometric church beautifully embodies the Tapatía school of architecture.

name: Museo de la Marimba (Marimba Museum)
description: This museum honors a unique musical instrument of African origins, whose sound defines a city.

name: Capilla de la Conchita (Church of the Immaculate Conception)
description: One of the oldest Christian churches still standing in the Americas, and almost certainly Mexico's oldest in continuous use.

name: Mundo Fútbol
description: What better place to learn about the beautiful game than an actual soccer ball-shaped building?

name: Levadura de Olla
description: Step past a rainbow of Oaxacan nightshades for a menu of regional moles worthy of weddings and funerals.

name: Rockdrigo Memorial
description: A statue in Balderas subway station honors the rock star who wrote about it in a song.

name: La Gran Puerta de México (Mexico's Great Gateway)
description: A "gateway" into Mexico's northern border, represented by an abstract red tower.

name: Casa China
description: A mysterious, abandoned Chinese building in Mexico pays homage to the area's East Asian immigrants.

name: Santiago Carbonell Foundation-Museum
description: An elegant space dedicated to a master of romanticism and minimalism.

name: Virgen Monumental de Xicotepec
description: This enormous statue of the Virgin of Guadalupe has become one of the world's most recent Catholic pilgrimage sites.

name: Mercado Abelardo L. Rodríguez Murals
description: Works from the golden age of Mexican muralism can be found inside a typical city market.

name: Galeria Atotonilco
description: Inside this gallery is amazing folk art from across Mexico.

name: 'Huellas de la Octava' ('Footprints of the Eighth')
description: A series of mosaics in Mexico City's Historic Center celebrate the Basque Country's "eighth province"—its diaspora.

name: Mina de Mármol
description: A former travertine mine traces the geological history of this unique desert filled with crystalline blue pools.

name: Ecumenical Chapel of Peace
description: A giant hilltop cross marks the location of a sanctuary with stunning views of Acapulco.

name: Árbol de Manitas (Little Hands Tree)
description: The curious flowers of this hard-to-find tree are shaped like little hands.

name: Iglesia de Santo Santiago Apóstol (Church of Saint James the Apostle)
description: Located in the shadow of a volcano, this church is tied to the culture of the Purépecha people.

name: Parroquia de Nuestra Señora de la Asunción (Parish of Our Lady of the Assumption)
description: This modernist church's massive sci-fi dome may be one of Latin America's largest.

name: Old Airport Control Tower
description: One of Mexico's youngest major cities still holds on to this structure representing its short modern history.

name: Querétaro Aqueduct
description: A beautiful example of 18th-century engineering still standing tall.

name: 'La Ceiba' Water Tank
description: The majestic, sacred tree is represented on this piece of public infrastructure.

name: La Frijolería
description: Dine on funerary beans alongside off-duty lucha libre wrestlers at this legume-centric spot near Museo Rufino Tamayo.

name: Jardín Botánico Regional de Cadereyta
description: An alluring, arid botanical garden home to a bewildering variety of cacti both endangered and common.

name: La Casa del que Mató al Animal
description: The scene of a legendary hunt is displayed on this building's facade.

name: Temple of San Nicolás Tolentino
description: After a partial collapse in the 1950s, this small church's historic facade lives on.

name: Plaza of the Lebanese Migrant
description: A coastal monument honors one of Mexico's most notable immigrant groups.

name: Museo Menonita (Mennonite Museum)
description: A place to learn about the history and culture of Mexico's largest Anabaptist group.

name: Museo de Historia Natural
description: Fossilized remains tell the story of the region’s past inside this small, unassuming museum.

name: Buque Cañonero C-07 Guanajuato (C-07 Guanajuato Gunship)
description: This flagship of the Mexican Navy has had a bumpy second life after being retired from service.

name: Posada el Castillo
description: The former home of surrealist Edward James, who gave Xilitla its main attraction.

name: Boeing 727 Crash Experiment
description: This mangled and twisted wreckage left by the side of a Mexican road was no accident, but a planned crash in the name of science and safety.

name: Museum of El Santo
description: An old railway building commemorates one of Mexico's most celebrated lucha libre stars.

name: Las Quince Letras
description: With more than 30 ingredients, chef Cecilia Florian’s stupendously complex, harmonious moles are a masterclass in layering flavors.

name: Casino Cosmopolita
description: This former hotel and casino was part of a paper mill operation that was one of Mexico's largest employers.

name: Tierra del Sol
description: Bold, unapologetically spicy Mixtecan flavors are on the menu here.

name: Museo de la Caricatura (Caricature Museum)
description: Celebrating the art of caricature, from comics to political cartoons.

name: Justo Sierra Historical Synagogue
description: Mexico’s first synagogue set up by and for the Ashkenazi Jewish community.

name: La Teca
description: A glamorous octogenarian chef-owner serves rare Istmeño specialties in a tropical garden.

name: Templo de San Juan Bautista (Temple of Saint John the Baptist)
description: Oaxaca's recently restored "forgotten Dominican convent."

name: Plaza Valentín Gómez Farías
description: Named after a Mexican president, who once lived nearby, this park in a residential Mexico City neighborhood packs a whole lot of history into a small space.

name: Aqueduct of Padre Tembleque
description: In the 16th century, a Franciscan monk pulled off a construction miracle by building a Roman-style aqueduct to save the town of Otumba, Mexico, from certain death.

name: Gran Stupa Bön Para la Paz Mundial
description: One of the oldest and largest Buddhist structures in the Americas, located in a peaceful forest outside Mexico City.

name: Santuario de Schoenstatt 'Maria, Camino al Cielo'
description: A mountaintop shrine named for the road to heaven.

name: Acteal Pillar of Shame
description: A 26-foot tall roadside concrete pillar recalls a yet-unresolved 1997 political massacre.

name: Caracol Chivita Murals
description: This series of paintings celebrate a locally beloved snail species.

name: Caesar’s Restaurant Bar
description: The original home of the Caesar Salad.

name: La Piedra del 68 (The Stone of '68)
description: This simple rock stands as a memorial of the major student protests of 1968.

name: Monterrey’s Contemporary Art Museum (MARCO)
description: The outdoor area of this contemporary art museum in Mexico is a modernist reinterpretation of hacienda architecture.

name: Catedral Basílica Menor de Durango (Durango Cathedral)
description: An eerie illusion of a nun is said to haunt this cathedral’s Baroque-style bell tower.

name: República de Guatemala 28
description: A trompe l'oeil mural remembers the original colonial building that once stood here.

name: Glorieta Plaza de Sevilla (Seville Plaza Roundabout)
description: A unique hybrid of fountain, stained glass and monument, designed and built by a notable architect-engineer team.

name: Monumento al Volador (Monument to the Flyer)
description: A massive yet whimsical statue pays homage to the art and joy of "flight."

name: Indios Verdes (Green Indians Monument)
description: After several moves across Mexico City, these two statues found a home in Mestizaje Park.

name: Ex-Convento Franciscano de Kikil
description: The ruins of a 16th-century Spanish convent tell a forgotten history.

name: Misión Santa Rosa de las Palmas
description: Once a Catholic mission, this small town tucked between the ocean and the mountains is now a magical spot for art and history lovers.

name: Ahuehuete de Chalma
description: This specimen of Mexico's national tree is now central to religious pilgrimage and rituals.

name: Emiliano Zapata House Museum
description: The ruins of the humble house of one of Mexico's most iconic revolutionaries.

name: Jorge Wilmot National Museum of Ceramics
description: A tribute to the region's rich history of pottery art.

name: Ecoducto Río La Piedad
description: A river runs beneath you while walking along this elevated linear park.

name: Rectoría del Señor de La Humildad (Manzanares Chapel)
description: Mexico City’s smallest church, designed as a miniature cathedral and visited mainly by robbers and prostitutes.

name: Real de Catorce
description: This former ghost town high in the mountains of northern Mexico is now a pilgrimage site.

name: Eagle Cuauhxicalli
description: This avian-shaped stone that once held the hearts of Aztec human sacrifices now lies at the feet of Christ inside a Catholic church.

name: Santuario de la Cruz Parlante (Sanctuary of the Speaking Cross)
description: This shrine is dedicated to a symbol of the Maya people's struggle against inequality and injustice.

name: ‘El Mural de la Conquista’ (‘Mural of the Conquest’)
description: In this state repository of folk art, perhaps no piece is more representative than this brutal work featuring the town’s distinctive black clay pottery.

name: Porta Coeli Cathedral
description: One of Mexico City's seats of the Catholic faith, although not the majoritarian Latin Roman rite.

name: Nahualac Pond
description: A shrine in the middle of this pond represents a Mesoamerican creation myth.

name: Museo de Arte Popular (Popular Art Museum)
description: This museum is dedicated to showcasing the collection of the Mama Viña y Oscar Foundation.

name: Korea-Mexico Friendship Bell
description: A replica of one of South Korea's National Treasures stands as a tribute to that country's relations with Mexico.

name: El Halconazo Memorial
description: An abstract sculpture memorializing a student massacre that took place in 1971.

name: Luz de Luna
description: Escape the city for a meal where deep-fried caterpillars, heirloom corn, and cult local mezcals shine.

name: Elena Garro Cultural Center
description: Named after and designed by women, this unique cultural center is an early 20th-century mansion covered in a modern shell.

name: Meliponicultura in Rancho Grande Valle Nacional
description: A small outpost dedicated to protecting the stingless bees native to Oaxaca.

name: Casa Cueva
description: Remnants of an architect's residence that displays his embrace of organic architecture.

name: Hotel Casa Grande Wall
description: A wall of this old hotel was decorated with drawings made of pebbles during its construction.

name: Edificio Celanese
description: This striking building appears to defy gravity, a tower balancing atop a much smaller cube.

name: House of Luis Cantón Marín
description: A house mixing elements of Chinese and Mexican architecture stands as a tribute to the Chinese-Mexican bishop who lived here.

name: Rosalío Solano Museum of National Cinema
description: Dedicated to one of the most iconic cinematographers of the Golden Age of Mexican Cinema.

name: Fuerte de San Miguel
description: A Spanish Colonial fort now houses an archeology museum.

name: Ho Chi Minh Monument
description: The Vietcong leader isn’t the only symbol of late 20th-century counterculture represented in this statue.

name: Chocolate 'OJERT' Mural Advert
description: A hand-painted advertisement for chocolate adorns the walls of this historic building.

name: Museo Cassa Gaia
description: Home to works by notable Mexican artists and an underwater mosaic of the pre-Columbian goddess Gaia.

name: Alfonsina
description: Venture outside of the city and into a family compound for a meal unlike any other.

name: The Tomb at Cuthá Mountain
description: The tomb of a pre-Hispanic governor, inside the vestiges of a Mesoamerican city.

name: Galería Juan Quezada
description: A small gallery in the home of the founding father of Mata Ortiz pottery.

name: Ex-Hacienda de Chautla
description: A historic Puebla estate featuring a striking castle.

name: Estatua del Pescador (The Fisherman's Statue)
description: This larger-than-life golden statue pays tribute to this town's old and new trades.

name: Plazuela de la Paz (Little Plaza of Peace)
description: A small site dedicated to the memory of a local leader and the search for peace.

name: "El Huracán de la Sierra" (Hurricane of the Sierra)
description: This truck is celebrated as part of the "miracle" that connected this town to the rest of Mexico.

name: Fuente de los Tres Sombreros
description: This hat-shaped fountain pays tribute to a centuries-old textile tradition in the Yucatán Peninsula.

name: Murals of the Government Palace
description: Twenty-four works by the last great Mexican muralist tell the story of Tlaxcala.

name: Casa Madero
description: This grape-growing paradise is one of the oldest vineyards in North America.

name: Parroquia del Purísimo Corazón de María Reina de la Paz
description: Featured in a Hollywood film, this church is a monumental example of mid-century art deco architecture.

name: San Vicente de Chupaderos
description: A small town that began as a western movie set.

name: Nocheztlicalli - Museo Ecológico de Grana Cochinilla
description: Here you can learn about an insect that was called "cactus blood," which trade turned immensely valuable as "red gold".

name: Las Coloradas Bridge
description: This small, yet notable, cable-stayed bridge was built to support tourism to a series of internet-famous pink lakes.

name: Monumento al Burro (Monument to the Donkey)
description: A farming family's tribute to the pack animal.

name: Museo del Vaquero de Las Californias
description: This bilingual museum explores the more than 300 years of vaquero history.

name: Péndulo de Tequisquiapan
description: This monument marking the center of Mexico might be more political than geographical.

name: Casa Cruz
description: Legacy of Isidoro Cruz Hernández, one of the most crucial artists in the history of the alebrijes craft in this town.

name: Museo Leyendas de Guanajuato
description: Miniatures built in the 1980s tell the city’s favorite legends and ghost stories.

name: Museo del Ferrocarril Antigua Estación del Golfo
description: A Beaux-Arts architectural surprise and a charming nostalgic tour through northern Mexico’s railway revolution.

name: Acueducto de Xochimilco
description: This aqueduct with 18th-century origins is integrated into modern life in various ways in Oaxaca.

name: Bacalar House of Culture
description: This city's cultural center is in a restored Spanish colonial home from the 1800s.

name: Ex Convent of Acolman
description: This 16th-century missionary site features Catholic murals, stone carvings, and fortress-like architecture.

name: ‘El Mural Que Debió Ser’ (‘The Mural That Should’ve Been’)
description: A modern feminist movement has brought to life the mural that machismo once denied to artist María Izquierdo.

name: Miniature Collection at Museo del Pueblo de Guanajuato
description: A collection of miniatures made in the central-western region of Mexico.

name: Plaza Aniceto Ortega del Villar
description: The heart of Pachuca's small museum district pays tribute to a unique figure of Hidalguense history.

name: Palo que Habla (Talking Stick)
description: When the wood used in the town's alebrijes crafts started to threaten the copal tree's sustainability, this reserve was set up in response.

name: 'Caracol de Vida' ('Snail of Life')
description: A monument in the shape of the mighty conch honors the history and symbolism behind this shell.

name: Museo De Arte Huichol Wixárika
description: A gallery displaying the psychedelic-fueled art of the indigenous Huichol people.

name: Plaza Tlaxcoaque
description: A small square that sheds its troubled recent history with its surrounding artworks, some of which are not without controversy.

name: Monumento de La Virgen Santísima de Guadalupe
description: After it was missing for a decade, this 40-foot statue of Our Lady of Guadalupe was found and returned to a place of honor—with a little help from a tropical storm.

name: Ex Cine Garza (Former Garza Cinema)
description: The building of this town's first purpose-built cinema theater now houses a restaurant, but its walls still represent a distant heritage.

name: Parque Los Conos
description: Abandoned pieces of Mexico’s agricultural industry are the centerpiece of this park.

name: Vitromurales de Zacatlán
description: This glass mural on the wall of a cemetery started a peculiar tradition in Zacatlán.

name: Coyote en Ayuno
description: A red steel coyote towering over one of Mexico City’s largest suburbs.

name: Fuente de las Ocho Regiones (Fountain of the 8 Regions)
description: The diversity of the state of Oaxaca is represented in this fountain.

name: Museo Arquidiocesano de Arte Sacro (Archdiocese Sacred Art Museum)
description: Its collection features not only Christian-themed art but also works made by members of the clergy.

name: Tree Shrine
description: In a town defined by its incredible trees, having a shrine in one just makes sense.

name: Coquitos of the Jalatlaco Church
description: Legend says that these centuries-old trees, which pre-date their nearby church, were planted by monks.

name: Museo Jtatik Samuel
description: This museum in Chiapas honors Bishop Samuel Ruiz García, a key figure of the Zapatista movement.

name: The Bastion of San Carlos
description: This historic, pentagon-shaped fort houses the City Museum.

name: Marisela Escobedo Memorial Plaque
description: This simple plaque remembers the fearless human rights defender's tragic death.

name: Former Train Station of Izamal
description: An abandoned train station still stands as a testament to the region's industrial and transport history.

name: Museo de la Batalla de Monterrey (Battle of Monterrey Museum)
description: This museum tells the story of a decisive battle in the Mexican-American War.

name: Museo de la Restauración de la República
description: This former convent is where Mexico’s last emperor awaited execution.

name: Old Penitentiary of Sonora
description: The site of Mexico’s last civilian execution is now a museum dedicated to regional history.

name: Well of Conquest
description: A well was built by the Spanish before their conquest of Mexico.

name: Museo Sergio Castro ‘YokChij’
description: A diverse collection from all over Chiapas, gathered by a philanthropist during his activism in the state.

name: Fuente de los Muñecos
description: They say that the sculptures in the fountain dedicated to deceased children move at night.

name: Jesús Malverde Chapel
description: A giant shrine to Mexico's "narco-saint."

name: MacPuente (McBridge)
description: This elevated footbridge has become a focal point for planespotting in Mexico City.

name: La Isla de las Muñecas (Island of the Dolls)
description: An island filled with hundreds of hanging, decomposing, decapitated dolls.

name: Giant Crystals of Naica
description: A  gargantuan crystal kingdom.

name: The Cake Room at Pastelería Ideal
description: Where layer cakes tower above one’s head and there is never a shortage of icing.

name: Future Forest
description: A colorful "forest" made entirely from three tons of recycled plastic waste.

name: Joyxee Island
description: One man's fulfilled dream of living on an artificial island made of recycled plastic bottles.

name: Gamers El Moro Video Game Museum
description: A curious museum of old video game consoles hidden in the back of a store.

name: Museo de Perversidad (Museum of Perversity)
description: Shocking dioramas of human torture, meant to promote human rights awareness.

name: Mexican Calendars Exhibition at the Soumaya Museum
description: This collection of promotional calendars preserves a staple of mid-century Mexican pop art.

name: Museum of Miniatures
description: Hidden inside a hotel is a museum of miniature models depicting the traditions and history of Mexico.

name: Futura CDMX
description: A detailed model of Mexico City is the star attraction within this interactive exhibit inside an old theater.
